narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your academic journey, including your Year 1 school decile of 6—solidly above the typical student's 5.7—and your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, which is a bit below the average of 3.2. Your LSAT score of 32 falls short of the usual 37, your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you've indicated plans to attend part-time, a choice only about 7% of students make. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your probability of failing at 43%, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of future bar struggles. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile of 1, far below the average 5.5, signaling a significant drop in performance that strongly outweighed other strengths for this model. On a more positive note, your stronger Year 1 decile pushed noticeably in favor of passing, showing the model recognized your early potential. Your undergraduate GPA also helped a little, nudging the odds toward success despite being under average. Compared to typical students who pass the bar on the first try, your later academic dip stood out as the dominant concern, while your part-time status and LSAT didn't sway things as much in this case. This isn't a judgment on your overall worth or determination—many factors like resilience and study habits play into bar success beyond these metrics. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted coursework or tutoring, and exploring full-time options if feasible to align with the majority profile. You might also seek feedback from pre-law advisors on bolstering your Year 3 record or gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your early promise. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view in your favor, and schools often value diverse backgrounds like yours when paired with improved indicators. Keep pushing—your upper-middle family support gives you a solid foundation to build on.
